steps.title: Identify the research problem
steps.content: The passage says ecologists have learned much about wild-bee declines from bumblebees because bumblebees are heavily researched. That creates a possible generalization problem: evidence from one well-studied genus may be used to understand declines across many other wild-bee genera.

steps.title: Use Moreau's key distinction
steps.content: Moreau points out that bumblebees have two traits that many wild bees do not share: social behaviors and dietary generalism. The passage also says those traits are associated with increased resilience to some specific environmental changes. That means bumblebees may not respond to every environmental threat in the same way other wild bees respond.

steps.title: Reject unsupported conclusions
steps.content: A makes a specific claim about bumblebees being more harmed by climate variation than by habitat destruction, but the passage does not rank those threats. B goes too far because it says researchers should not use bumblebees to draw conclusions even about similar bees. C turns a limited resilience point into a broad prediction about future population changes.

steps.title: Draw the cautious inference
steps.content: The logical conclusion should preserve both parts of the passage: bumblebee research is important, but bumblebees have traits that can make them different from other wild bees. Because their responses to threats are not always comparable, researchers should be careful when extending conclusions from bumblebees to wild-bee declines more broadly. The answer is D.
